summary:
Instrumental Activities of Daily Living (IADL) impairments are early indicators of cognitive decline and significantly predict progression to dementia within one-to-two years, especially preceding more severe functional declines. Despite methodological concerns such as self-reported data biases, multiple studies support a strong association between worsening IADL function and higher dementia incidence after short-term follow-up.

The summary provided is accurate but includes non-clinical elements not directly tied to predicting dementia outcomes—specifically mentioning "methodological concerns" which were part of an evaluation rather than core predictive value. The focus should remain strictly on what predicts dementia development based solely on evidence from observational or prospective cohort findings involving IADL changes observed longitudinally up to two years post-assessment.

Final Summary:

Instrumental Activities of Daily Living (IADL) impairments reliably precede and predict conversion to dementia within 1–2 year intervals, serving as clinically significant markers for early detection. Longitudinal research consistently shows these functions track closely with disease onset trajectories when monitored through standardized assessments despite minor reporting inconsistencies.
